*Includes people in prison for new offenses and with sentence lengths that are not less than one year or life sentences.


Parole EligibilityPrison PopulationPrison ReleasesDisparities




Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most people in prison earn one day of credit for each day served. Credits are deducted from judicial maximum terms to produce earlier mandatory release dates. So-called “indeterminate” sentences carry minimum and maximum terms that are specified in statute and cannot be varied by the court when imposing a prison sentence. There is no standard ratio between minimum and maximum terms. The other sentence type “definite sentences” include terms to parole release eligibility that are set by statutory formula rather than the judicial sentence; the default is parole-release eligibility after 25 percent.







In 2020, 43 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility.








From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 36 percent.















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2020, 88 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people.










In 2020, 89 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males.










In 2020, 42 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 26 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 69 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (39%) and public order (18%) offenses.















In 2020, 62 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

From 2010 to 2022, the prison population decreased 12 percent, changing from 6,681 in 2010 to 5,847 in 2022.
















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2022, 84 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic people.










In 2022, 90 percent of people in prison were males.










In 2022, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 23 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for property offenses.















In 2020, 41 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2010 to 2020, prison releases increased 13 percent, dropping from 2,970 in 2010 to 3,342 in 2020.









In 2020, 41 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 34 percent increase compared to 2010.















Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions.

In 2020, 84 percent of prison releases were males.










In 2020, 39 percent of prison releases were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 38 percent of prison releases were for people incarcerated for property offenses.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.









Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp; “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program.

States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For most felonies people in prison become eligible for discretionary parole release after they have served 50 percent of the sentence imposed by the court.







In 2020, 27 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility.








From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent.















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2020, 46 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people.










In 2020, 92 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males.










In 2020, 37 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 47 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 52 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for property (24%) and public order (21%) offenses.















In 2020, 48 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 to 4.9 years.

In 2020, 19 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for aggravated or simple assault offenses.















In 2020, 23 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.













From 2010 to 2020, prison releases decreased 9 percent, dropping from 10,570 in 2010 to 9,632 in 2020.









In 2020, 67 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 14 percent increase compared to 2010.

While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

title: Arnold Ventures Parole Project
text:
The Council of State Governments (CSG) Justice Center intends to illuminate parole release policies and practices in the United States and how they impact prison population sizes and lengths of stay, using data from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) from up to 45 states to:

Examine trends in their parole-eligible populations;
Conduct original empirical research on racial, ethnic, and gender disparities on the portion of sentences that are served in carceral settings past parole eligibility; and
Conduct deep dives into selected states to shed light on parole board decision-making. For these states, we will seek to identify any disparate decision-making and associated outcomes and recommend changes to policy and practice to prevent unnecessarily long prison stays while maintaining public safety.

Parole Eligibility Data by State
This table provides a detailed projection of the 2023 snapshot of state-level prison populations*, including the percentage and number of individuals incarcerated beyond their parole eligibility. It also highlights the number of parole board members for each state.







*Includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.The number of parole board members includes appointed members and does not include subordinate hearing examiners that many states employ. Louisiana is listed among the states with parole systems, despite its recent abolition of parole, due to a substantial population of individuals who remain eligible for parole release under the previous system.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
people in prison convicted of nonviolent crimes with sentences of more than six months become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-fourth of their judicial maximum sentence. people in prison convicted of an offense statutorily classified as a “violent crime” become eligible for discretionary parole release after serving one-half of the maximum.







In 2020, 55 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility.








From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility decreased by 2 percent.















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2020, 68 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic people.










In 2020, 97 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males.










In 2020, 46 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 67 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 31 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for robbery (25%) and aggravated or simple assault (22%) offenses.















In 2020, 54 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.

State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Montana is one of a small number of states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.

title: Montana
text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
For general-rules people in prison, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of incarcerated people’s judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people in prison will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a small number of states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.

text:
Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Most people in prison are serving “determinate” sentences but a substantial number are serving “indeterminate” sentences. Determinate sentences are for higher levels of seriousness and they receive a judicial maximum that can only be reduced with credits topping out at 14 percent discount. Indeterminate sentences receive a judicial minimum and maximum with no statutory constraint on the ratio between them. people in prison serving indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole “at any time” after the expiration of their minimum sentences which may be advanced by as much as 17 percent for merit time credits.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
The Oklahoma parole board has authority to grant discretionary parole release only to people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses. For people in prison convicted of “violent offenses” the parole board is limited only to making recommendations to release to the governor. The release decision itself must be made by the governor; it is the only state where the governor is the ultimate decision-maker in ordinary parole-release cases for large numbers of people in prison. For offenses committed between July 1, 1998 and November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after serving one-third of their judicial maximum sentences. For offenses committed on or after November 1, 2018 people in prison become eligible after one-fourth of their judicial maximum terms. people in prison convicted of nonviolent offenses are eligible for more streamlined “administrative parole.” Violent offenses are subject to 85 percent eligibility. Earned credits can reach 60 days per month for nonviolent and 44 days per month for violent.

text:
While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material.
State data broken down by race and ethnicity were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals.
States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. The US Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and Federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people.

Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person’s prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison.
Sentencing courts have wide discretion to vary the degree of indeterminacy in a sentence with minimums allowed to range up to 90 percent of the maximum. Good time credits can achieve a one-third reduction (15 days/month) in a mandatory release date and advance parole eligibility.







In 2020, 39 percent of people in prison were in prison past their parole eligibility.








From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility increased by 30 percent.















Race and EthnicityGenderAge


In 2020, 77 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic people.










In 2020, 87 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were males.










In 2020, 36 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 to 34 years old.


















In 2020, 44 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 54 percent for non-violent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for drugs (27%) and rape or sexual assault (24%) offenses.















In 2020, 42 percent of people* in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 to 24.9 years.
